items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: What distinction matters for the free will defence to the problem of evil?
items.correct: Between moral evil (caused by free human choices) and natural evil (earthquakes, disease) — the free will defence directly addresses moral evil but struggles with natural evil, which isn't a product of free choice.
items.misconception: The free will defence solves all forms of evil at once.
items.random_wrong: Natural evil means evil that is good for nature.
items.why: Students apply the free will defence to all evil. The challenge of natural evil is precisely that no one chose it, requiring other theodicies (Augustinian fallen world, Irenaean soul-making) to address it.

items.q: What is substance dualism about mind and body?
items.correct: The view (associated with Descartes) that mind and body are two distinct kinds of substance — a non-physical mind interacting with a physical body.
items.misconception: Substance dualism is the belief that humans have two personalities.
items.random_wrong: Substance dualism means men and women are different.
items.why: Students misread 'dualism' as referring to two of anything. It specifically refers to two kinds of substance (mental and physical) — a metaphysical position with deep philosophical implications.

items.q: Is Q a document scholars possess?
items.correct: No — Q (from German Quelle, 'source') is a hypothesis: a posited sayings source explaining the material Matthew and Luke share but Mark lacks; no manuscript of it exists.
items.misconception: Q is a recently discovered papyrus kept in the Vatican library.
items.random_wrong: Q is another name for the Gospel of Thomas.
items.why: Because Q is inferential, alternatives like the Farrer hypothesis (Luke used Matthew) remain live. Students who call Q a discovered text misstate the entire basis of the two-source theory.

items.q: Does close verbal agreement between the synoptics prove they are independent eyewitness reports?
items.correct: No — the opposite: extensive word-for-word agreement in Greek is the classic evidence of literary dependence, i.e. that the evangelists used common written sources.
items.misconception: The synoptics agree word-for-word because three independent eyewitnesses remembered events identically.
items.random_wrong: The synoptics never agree with each other in wording.
items.why: Independent memories do not produce identical Greek sentences. Verbatim agreement is precisely why scholars posit Markan priority and Q — the foundational logic of the synoptic problem.
The answer examiners want is: Matthew, Mark, Luke. “Matthew, Mark, John” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Q stands for the German word:
items.why: Correct answer: Quelle. “Quaerere” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many double-tradition verses are usually attributed to Q?
items.why: Exact answer: 235. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
